dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a transfer/interfacility call at the VA. Suspected stroke with receptive and expressive aphasia.
Hi, I'm EMT Alex and this is my partner Brad. We've been called to transfer a patient to [COMPANY_NAME] today. Can you tell us a bit about what brought you here?
Hi, I'm a VA staff member. We called for help because the patient is experiencing symptoms of an ischemic stroke and needs a higher level of care.
The patient is a 77-year-old male who was last known well at noon today. He developed right-sided facial droop, right-sided weakness, and aphasia (trouble speaking and understanding).
Patient is conscious and breathing, but not fully oriented. He's experiencing expressive and receptive aphasia. Let me check his responsiveness.
He opens his eyes spontaneously. That's a score of 4 on the GCS Eye portion.
He replies incoherently and repeats phrases. That gives him a verbal score of 4 on the GCS.
When we asked him to squeeze our fingers, he localized to the pain but didn't follow verbal commands.
Brad, can you confirm the GCS as documented?
GCS is 13. Eye 4, Verbal 4, Motor 5.
Bystander, is there any airway compromise?
No airway compromise observed. Breathing is regular, and the airway remains patent.
Chest rise is equal. Let me auscultate. Breath sounds are clear throughout all lobes.
No signs of cyanosis or labored breathing. Chest appears to expand symmetrically.
Brad, check pulses and skin.
Pulses are strong and regular. Skin is warm, pink, and dry.
Can you tell us when this event began and what preceded it?
He was last known well at noon. At the VA, he showed right-sided facial droop, weakness, and aphasia.
Does he have any known medical conditions?
He has a history of hypertension and atrial fibrillation.
What did he complain of before this stroke?
He mentioned headache, though communication is difficult due to aphasia.
He received tPA at the VA at 1317. Facial droop improved, but mild right-sided weakness and aphasia remain.
How old is the patient and what is his weight?
He's 77 and weighs 113 kg.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how severe is his reported or observed pain?
His pain is rated at 8. He appeared notably distressed with headache.
Does the pain appear to radiate to any other areas?
It's primarily in the head, with no reported radiation to other areas.
Checking for DCAPBTLS in the head and neck. I do not see any signs of trauma.
Head is intact and no trauma noted in the neck.
Based on the clinical presentation and tPA use, does the VA documentation mention a possible middle cerebral artery occlusion?
Yes, documentation states possible middle cerebral artery occlusion.
Chest is clear, with equal rise and clear breath sounds bilaterally.
Right-sided mild weakness noted. No specific deformities or angulations observed in extremities.
No issues observed on the back, flank, or buttocks.
Pulse is 76, regular.
Respiratory rate is 26.
Blood pressure stands at 174 over 92.
Blood glucose is 96.
Oxygen saturation is 99%.
IV has been placed using a 22-gauge needle in his right forearm as per stroke protocol.
A 20-gauge IV is also in the left forearm for backup infusion access.
Can you place the ECG leads on the patient now, Brad?
ECG monitoring is active. Initial reading is stable with no acute issues.
We're following the medical stroke/TIA protocol, as outlined in Protocol 3-5.
Heart rate has increased to 85.
Blood pressure is elevated to 197 over 69.
Respiratory rate remains at 26.
Oxygen saturation is the same at 99%.
EKG shows no changes from initial readings.
We will transport the patient to [COMPANY_NAME] as requested by the VA. No other facility is indicated.
Brad, help me secure the patient in the stretcher using a five-point harness.
Ready for transport. Patient is safely secured and ready to move.
All necessary equipment is in place and vitals are being closely monitored.
Upon arrival, the patient will be transferred directly to CCH 10th floor, room 104A via stretcher.
A hospital bed is ready in the CCH unit.
Patient was transferred over to the bed using a sheet lift.
All patient information and interventions have been documented for handover.
Patient care has been handed off to the CCH nurse.
EMS is clear to move on, and transport documents are finalized.
ECGRAM shows normal sinus rhythm with no signs of acute myocardial infarction.
All safety checks are complete. Patient vitals are trending normally.
Transport route has been confirmed. GPS destination is CCH 10th floor emergency room.
Monitor is set to alert if there are any drops in blood pressure or heart rate during transport.
All medications have been tagged at the VA point-of-care and are ready for nursing verification.
Transfer report has been handed off to the receiving nurse and signs off acceptable.
Skin is warm, intact, and dry with no signs of rashes, hives, or bruising.
Nose and mouth clear with no obstruction, no drainage or hemorrhage noted.
Abdominal exam reveals normal contour and no signs of distension or tenderness.
Neck is supple with no signs of bruising, swelling, or tenderness upon palpation.
No signs of ear trauma. No bruising or discharge. Normal gross inspection.
Ready to load the patient into the ambulance. No anticipated complications during transfer.
All equipment secured in ambulance bay-ready for departure.
Head is clear of DCAPBTLS. Positive facial droop initially, now minimal with full GCS.
No oral trauma, swelling, or foreign bodies. Dentition normal on inspection.
Upon reaching the hospital, will receive care directly in the stroke unit by [COMPANY_NAME] neuro team.
EKG remains fully stable compared to initial reading with no signs of emergent arrhythmias.
No signs of penoscrotal or scrotal edema, discoloration, or discharge.
Had the patient eaten anything before this event?
Not recorded day he was last known well. No oral intake documented.
Airway remains patent, no signs of secretions or compromise observed.
Sheet lift will occur on the stretcher to preserve protocol and avoid extra movement.
Patient secured with straps and harness on stretcher and is ready for load into ambulance.
Final read at 19:28 shows stable vitals with consistent values for transfer.
